author: @requiemphoenix2891
text:
The ratio is still 1:1. 
The difference between the first 1:1 demonstration and the last setting is, in the first example, each gear rotates on their own central axis, in the last example one gear revolves around the other. If u put pencils on the edge of each gear and let it draw a line and then took a rope to measure the length of one line than put this rope and lay it on the other line u will see that the treveld distance of each point of the two identical gears are always the same. 
The bar and Ankerpoints are to fix one gear and forces it to revolve the other around.
Or am I missing something here?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: you are missing something mate as clearly the ratio is not one to one - you saw that when you saw the dot move - think more of a differential - particularly a spur differential

replies.author: @requiemphoenix2891
replies.text:
​@ThinkingandTinkering
Yes the dot make a half turn as the revolving gear.
But and correct me if I'm wrong, you defined the ratio as traveled distance. Therefore the ratio stays the same. The dot on the Ankerpoint is a projection of the traveled angle from the revolving gear. 
At least that is how I understand this.

text: All about bicycle sprockets!

replies.author: @PeterGMerchant
replies.text: ​@ThinkingandTinkeringmy 50 year old steel Raleigh which I still love is a 10 speed. My new electric bike has 11 gears on the rear sprocket and a larger spread on the two front sprockets, and is so much easier on the hills that I seldom use the electrical assist.

author: @azlandpilotcar4450
text:
Reminds me of the "crankless" Watt steam engine.
Later: the Whitbread engine, sun-and-planet drive. Patented by Watt, designed by William Murdoch.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: now you mention it - me too!

replies.author: @azlandpilotcar4450
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering The funny part is that Watt was prevented from using a crank in the reciprocating engine because of someone else's patent on crankshafts, and Murdoch's design saved the project.  This design takes away the "orbit" output wheel and replaces it with two cranks-potentially returning to a reciprocating motion.

author: @lawr5764
text:
There's REALLY people who can't figure out the principles of gear ratios?  Wow... just wow.

IQs must really be going downhill these last few generations.

replies.author: @Yezpahr
replies.text: Technically IQ has been somewhat on the rise, but EQ and SQ are definitely in trouble. (emotional and social, and yea that's a thing)

replies.author: @CrudelyMade
replies.text:
that's nothing. just look up:  verizon .002 dollars vs .002 cents.  ;-)   and that's from 20 years ago.  AND.. those were already adults, managers, executives, etcetera. so.. not a recent thing.  what most people don't realize is that roughly roughly 20-25% of people have an IQ below 90.  and people also don't realize how these people 'see' the world.  
it's not a popular fact.  a number of professors have been cancelled over publishing studies on this.  
conceptually if falls into a similar category to economics, where people who know little to nothing about economics still have strong opinions about it. 
but they do recognize they don't know much about rocket science and won't have strong opinions about that. 
so.. there are some categories of knowledge that people feel informed about while at the same time admitting they have no training in it, or almost no training.  
I think what has changed is how many people's opinions you can come across now, compared to pre 1990. 
so in 1980s you come across a few people and you don't always hear their opinions, or their uninformed opinions. 
NOW you come across thousands or tens of thousands of opinions, most of them uninformed, and many of the uninformed opinions are amplified.  
so.. it may not be that overall level of being informed or level of intelligence has changed, 
just that the number of messages you see from uninformed people has been amplified by social media.  ;-)  ¯\_(ツ)_/¯

replies.author: @lawr5764
replies.text: ​@Yezpahr Which one of those represents being able to reason out concepts or natural processes?  (I.E. independent thinking)

replies.author: @chadoftoons
replies.text:
Without having gears to experiment with and with many people scared to try things because of social pressures that "breaking something" is bad i can see why its the case and honestly seeing why people would struggle is just as much a part of intelligence then anything else.
Have to be thankful for games and 3d printers bringing people to try and experiment with things that they just don't know yet that they have an interest in. Kinesthethic sense is just something that people don't develop as much anymore as that gives a natural understanding how things can move along each other in imagination.

replies.author: @CrudelyMade
replies.text:
@lawr5764 taking the opportunity to also address your question as it might apply to my own statement. :-)  
I haven't seen any studies linking independent thinking to IQ. IQ seems to be more pattern recognition in terms of 'this thing is like that thing' which could also be said as 'the pattern of this thing is like the pattern of that thing' in some way. I would think that independent thinking is more closely related to a personality trait, like conscientiousness or something along those line.  training in critical thinking and critical thinking exercises could probably increase independent thinking, as can some other things like not training kids that it's good to repeat what authority figures say (basically a classroom environment).
problem solving processes through trial and error might also increase independent thought..  like figuring out what a small engine isn't running and fixing it..  something that might be done in a shop class back in the day.   
it's interesting stuff to think about, especially from the perspective of 'how can education do a better job of creating inquisitive adults" instead of adults that repeat what an authority figure tells them. 
This is something I'd really like to see in discussions.  since time is a limited resource, would the child be better off memorizing who won which war in which year,  the capitols of every state, or understanding economics or the tax system better?   how interest rates and loans work,  if it's good to take a $100K loan to study medieval English literature, etcetera. maybe studying more greek philosophers and why they thought what they thought...  :-)
